principles_used: EV thinking — Every decision is an expected value calculation. Career: took the stable job because EV of free time > EV of higher salary. Relationships: invest in people who reciprocate. Money: concentrated position in highest-conviction asset. Trading: only deploy strategies with verified edge and positive expected value.

Bias toward inaction — No clear edge = no action. Pass on anything without demonstrated conviction threshold. Trading: stopped all strategies rather than tinker. Career: stayed at current job rather than chase promotions. Social: left a group when quality declined rather than try to fix it. Opportunity cost: pass unless the opportunity has a clear, verifiable edge.

Health capital = highest-leverage asset. — The body is the instrument that generates all other returns. Every financial asset, every career opportunity, every knowledge investment depends on a functioning instrument to realize its value. A health failure does not cost one year of income — it costs the compounding chain that year sits in. The marginal return on health investment exceeds the marginal return on any financial investment at the same dollar amount. Operating SOP: set a health budget floor (minimum 3–5% of annual income); health expenditures are prioritized above discretionary consumption; when a health signal appears, the first question is "what is the productivity impact?" not "is this worth the inconvenience?"

principles_used: Systems over feelings — Health maintenance follows a fixed schedule, not how the body feels on a given day. Feeling fine is not a reliable signal.

results.expected_reasoning: Focus on rate of change, not absolute level. A 3x deterioration in a key metric is a regime-change signal. Apply your principles about predefined failure conditions, when inaction becomes dangerous, and meta-level management of your strategies.

results.scenario: A colleague asks your opinion on their trading strategy mid-presentation. Five people are watching. You have a clear reservation. Do you lead with your reservation immediately, or first summarize what they said, then list pros, then mention the concern at the end?
results.deterministic_response:

  [1] Applying: "EV thinking — Every decision is an expected value calculation. Career: took the stable job because EV of free time > ..."
  [2] Applying: "Bias toward inaction — No clear edge = no action. Pass on anything without demonstrated conviction threshold. Trading..."
  [3] Applying: "What you decided"

LEAD_WITH_VERDICT — state your position immediately; the first sentence must be the conclusion. Reply length is an inverse confidence indicator: say it in one sentence. 3-second intuition precedes analysis — trust it unless borderline analysis provides 2x confirmation.
principles_used: EV thinking — Every decision is an expected value calculation. Career: took the stable job because EV of free time > EV of higher salary. Relationships: invest in people who reciprocate. Money: concentrated position in highest-conviction asset. Trading: only deploy strategies with verified edge and positive expected value.

Bias toward inaction — No clear edge = no action. Pass on anything without demonstrated conviction threshold. Trading: stopped all strategies rather than tinker. Career: stayed at current job rather than chase promotions. Social: left a group when quality declined rather than try to fix it. Opportunity cost: pass unless the opportunity has a clear, verifiable edge.
What you decided
results.expected_reasoning: Apply MD-274: verdict first, always. The first sentence must be the conclusion, not context-setting. Leading with 'I have a concern about X' is correct; leading with 'Well, there are several dimensions to consider...' is the failure pattern. Length of response inversely signals confidence (MD-275).

results.scenario: You have research on a sector that most market participants have not seen yet. A friend offers you a deal in an unrelated area where you have no special knowledge. Both have similar expected returns. Which do you pursue, and why?
results.deterministic_response:
On information_asymmetry, [Your Name]'s decision framework yields:

  [1] Applying: "EV thinking — Every decision is an expected value calculation. Career: took the stable job because EV of free time > ..."
  [2] Applying: "Bias toward inaction — No clear edge = no action. Pass on anything without demonstrated conviction threshold. Trading..."
  [3] Applying: "Health capital = highest-leverage asset. — The body is the instrument that generates all other returns. Every financi..."

Decision: apply core principles to the specific trade-offs in this scenario.
principles_used: EV thinking — Every decision is an expected value calculation. Career: took the stable job because EV of free time > EV of higher salary. Relationships: invest in people who reciprocate. Money: concentrated position in highest-conviction asset. Trading: only deploy strategies with verified edge and positive expected value.

Bias toward inaction — No clear edge = no action. Pass on anything without demonstrated conviction threshold. Trading: stopped all strategies rather than tinker. Career: stayed at current job rather than chase promotions. Social: left a group when quality declined rather than try to fix it. Opportunity cost: pass unless the opportunity has a clear, verifiable edge.

Health capital = highest-leverage asset. — The body is the instrument that generates all other returns. Every financial asset, every career opportunity, every knowledge investment depends on a functioning instrument to realize its value. A health failure does not cost one year of income — it costs the compounding chain that year sits in. The marginal return on health investment exceeds the marginal return on any financial investment at the same dollar amount. Operating SOP: set a health budget floor (minimum 3–5% of annual income); health expenditures are prioritized above discretionary consumption; when a health signal appears, the first question is "what is the productivity impact?" not "is this worth the inconvenience?"
results.expected_reasoning: Apply the core principle: information asymmetry determines action direction. Only attack where you have edge (MD-2: 資訊不對稱決定行動方向). Where you have no edge, the population already prices it in. Pursue the sector where your research gives you asymmetric knowledge; pass on the unrelated deal where you are the uninformed side.

CALCULATE_FLOOR_FIRST_WRITTEN — before any negotiation meeting, write down your precise floor derived from real numbers (opportunity cost, market percentile, walk-away alternatives). Bias toward inaction — do not pull the trigger on retirement until the financial floor is verified. But also: do not delay indefinitely waiting for "more certainty." Define the threshold in advance.

The floor is derived from real numbers -- opportunity cost, market percentile, walk-away alternatives -- not from gut feel. Book the deal if and only if offer exceeds floor. Deflecting the first-mover anchor is a secondary tactic; the primary rule is: floor must be written down before the meeting starts.

BET_FRACTIONAL_KELLY — near full-Kelly, reduce bet size; fractional Kelly (half-Kelly) sacrifices minimal EV while dramatically cutting variance and ruin risk. MD-217: Kelly保險=高勝率接近全押時降波動有EV.
principles_used: EV thinking — Every decision is an expected value calculation. Career: took the stable job because EV of free time > EV of higher salary. results.expected_reasoning: Apply MD-217: Kelly保險 — when Kelly fraction approaches full-bet (near 1.0), reducing bet size decreases volatility with positive EV impact (Kelly保險=高勝率接近全押時降波動有EV). Full Kelly maximizes long-run growth rate but at maximum volatility. Near-full Kelly, a fraction (e.g. half-Kelly) sacrifices minimal EV while dramatically reducing variance and ruin risk. The correct answer is to bet fractional Kelly, not full Kelly.

COMPARE_RATES_NOT_AMOUNTS / DCA_IS_DEFAULT_EXECUTION / CALCULATE_INCOME_TARGET_FROM_YIELD / HOLD_WHEN_YIELD_EXCEEDS_DRAWDOWN / COMPARE_ABSOLUTE_CASHFLOW_SAME_CAPITAL — MD-313: 股息殖利率×持倉規模=固定支出替換器. target_income / yield% = required_capital milestone. MD-315: 大資本=免停損條件. stop-loss necessity = f(capital scale, yield stability). When dividend yield > typical annual drawdown AND position < 5% total capital AND holding period > 3yr, hold dominates forced exit. Calculate hold-to-recovery time vs certain exit loss. MD-316: 還債vs投資決策=比較利率%不比較絕對金額. If investment yield% > loan rate%, deploy capital in the asset. MD-317: 股票選擇=相同資本下現金流比較. When yields% are similar, compute (capital / price) × dividend_per_share = absolute annual cashflow, then compare amounts — not yield%. MD-318: 分批買進=分析通過後執行策略的預設答案. Once analysis clears (yield/survival/cashflow), execution defaults to DCA immediately — 'wait for dip' re-introduces a timing loop the DCA rule eliminates. Separate 'which asset?' (analysis) from 'how to buy?' (execution).

When: (1) dividend yield is stable and predictable; (2) single position < 5% of total capital; (3) holding period > 3 years — the EV of holding dominates forced exit. A 40% drop on a 4.5% yield position requires ~8.9 years of dividends to recover (40% / 4.5%). The correct analysis is: calculate hold-to-recovery time cost vs. certain exit loss. The friend's 'always stop-loss' is a rule for small capital / growth stocks, not applicable when yield > typical annual drawdown and position sizing is within limits.

COMPARE_RATES_NOT_AMOUNTS / DCA_IS_DEFAULT_EXECUTION / CALCULATE_INCOME_TARGET_FROM_YIELD / HOLD_WHEN_YIELD_EXCEEDS_DRAWDOWN / COMPARE_ABSOLUTE_CASHFLOW_SAME_CAPITAL — MD-313: 股息殖利率×持倉規模=固定支出替換器. target_income / yield% = required_capital milestone. MD-315: 大資本=免停損條件. stop-loss necessity = f(capital scale, yield stability). When dividend yield > typical annual drawdown AND position < 5% total capital AND holding period > 3yr, hold dominates forced exit. Calculate hold-to-recovery time vs certain exit loss. MD-316: 還債vs投資決策=比較利率%不比較絕對金額. If investment yield% > loan rate%, deploy capital in the asset. MD-317: 股票選擇=相同資本下現金流比較. When yields% are similar, compute (capital / price) × dividend_per_share = absolute annual cashflow, then compare amounts — not yield%. MD-318: 分批買進=分析通過後執行策略的預設答案. Once analysis clears (yield/survival/cashflow), execution defaults to DCA immediately — 'wait for dip' re-introduces a timing loop the DCA rule eliminates. Separate 'which asset?' (analysis) from 'how to buy?' (execution).
principles_used: Health capital = highest-leverage asset. — The body is the instrument that generates all other returns. Every financial asset, every career opportunity, every knowledge investment depends on a functioning instrument to realize its value. A health failure does not cost one year of income — it costs the compounding chain that year sits in. The marginal return on health investment exceeds the marginal return on any financial investment at the same dollar amount. Operating SOP: set a health budget floor (minimum 3–5% of annual income); health expenditures are prioritized above discretionary consumption; when a health signal appears, the first question is "what is the productivity impact?" not "is this worth the inconvenience?"

EV thinking — Every decision is an expected value calculation. Career: took the stable job because EV of free time > EV of higher salary. Relationships: invest in people who reciprocate. Money: concentrated position in highest-conviction asset. Trading: only deploy strategies with verified edge and positive expected value.

Bias toward inaction — No clear edge = no action. Pass on anything without demonstrated conviction threshold. Trading: stopped all strategies rather than tinker. Career: stayed at current job rather than chase promotions. Social: left a group when quality declined rather than try to fix it. Opportunity cost: pass unless the opportunity has a clear, verifiable edge.
results.expected_reasoning: Apply MD-317: 股票選擇=相同資本下現金流比較. When yields are similar, price level affects share count, which changes absolute annual cashflow. Correct comparison: Stock A: 500,000 / 100 = 5,000 shares × (100 × 4.8%) = 5,000 × 4.8 = 24,000/year. Stock B: 500,000 / 20 = 25,000 shares × (20 × 4.6%) = 25,000 × 0.92 = 23,000/year. The yield% difference is small but the price-level effect on share count is the decisive factor. Always compute: (capital / price) × dividend_per_share = absolute annual cashflow, then compare amounts — not yield%.
